answer:
Andreas (cynewulf-andreas-07b573, chapter 1) frames the cosmos as a single, eternal divine order under Christ, who 'encompasses all by His craft alone, heaven and earth, with holy might' and who 'raised the heavens and fastened them firm with His own hands.' The order imposed on pagan Mermedonia is not negotiated but absolute: Christ is 'the one eternal God of all creation,' a sovereign whose commands bind the sea, silence the flood, and shatter idols. The clearest statement of this cosmic frame comes at paragraph p-355089 ('He is the rightful King, Wielder and Worker / of wondrous glory, the one eternal God / of all creation') and is confirmed at the poem's close in paragraph p-be1571, where the converted Mermedonians cry: 'One is the eternal God of all creation! / His might and His majesty over middle-earth / are famously blessed.' The frame is linear and sovereign: disorder is pagan; Christ's order supersedes and replaces it.

Manusmrti chapter 1 (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, chapter 1) frames the cosmos as cyclical and self-generating. The most direct cosmological statement is Mn_1.52 / paragraph p-946051: 'When that divine one is awake, then this world is active; when he, the tranquil-souled, sleeps, then the whole universe sinks into slumber.' This is immediately crowned by Mn_1.57 / paragraph p-4cb590: 'Thus he, the imperishable one, by means of waking and sleeping, incessantly vivifies and destroys this entire movable and immovable universe.' Dharma itself moves in cycles: Mn_1.81–1.82 (paragraphs p-fcafde, p-1365cd) state that in the Krita age Dharma is four-footed and complete, but diminishes by one foot in each succeeding age. The frame is rhythmic and emanative: creation is not a one-time act of a sovereign will overriding chaos, but a breathing in and out of the Absolute.

Which verse survives translation into the other's idiom? The Andreas verse that translates most cleanly into Manu's idiom is p-355089: 'He is the rightful King… the one eternal God of all creation. He encompasses all by His craft alone, heaven and earth.' The concept of an all-encompassing, self-existent Lord who creates and sustains heaven and earth maps directly onto the Manusmrti's Svayambhu (Self-existent One) from Mn_1.6 (p-93cef0). Strip the Old English heroic register ('King', 'thanes', 'craft') and the Manusmrti's philosophical vocabulary absorbs the claim without remainder.

The Manusmrti verse that translates most cleanly into Andreas's idiom is Mn_1.57 / p-4cb590: 'he, the imperishable one, by means of waking and sleeping, incessantly vivifies and destroys this entire movable and immovable universe.' Andreas's poet already uses a near-equivalent: Christ 'holds the power of life, He who binds the seas / the brown waves, who tames and threatens them' (p-f3580c). The cyclical vivification-and-destruction of Mn_1.57 can be recast in the Andreas idiom as the Lord who calms the sea one moment and floods the city the next — omnipotent alternation is structurally identical even if Manu's frame is cosmic time and Andreas's is dramatic narrative. The deep incompatibility is not in the cosmic-creator claim but in directionality: Andreas's order is eschatological and one-directional (pagan disorder ends, Christian order is permanent); Manu's is cyclic (dharma will rise again after Kali Yuga). Neither verse, translated, can carry that asymmetry into the other's idiom.
